name: Platform-Neutral Standards Engagement
consequences: Engaging EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards, making supplier-neutral design harder to exclude. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Lead negotiations with EU bodies to include platform-neutrality requirements in Danish digital identity planning
Develop and publish open standards for fallback authentication that reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Build relationships with key stakeholders within the European Digital Identity Wallet framework to influence procurement decisions
review: This lever governs the tension between platform-neutrality and existing infrastructure, but the options overlook potential pushback from incumbent operators.

name: Procurement Intervention
consequences: Influencing procurement processes around AltID will create a demand for platform-neutral solutions, making it more difficult for suppliers to lock-in users. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop and publish procurement language that requires Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
Work with procurement stakeholders to ensure AltID requirements include platform-neutrality and fallback capabilities
Engage with suppliers to develop platform-neutral solutions that can be integrated into existing infrastructure
review: This lever governs the tension between procurement influence and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

name: Demonstrator Development
consequences: Developing non-production technical demonstrators will provide credible technical evidence that platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models are feasible. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards
Explore a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether
Focus on developing a single, feature-rich demonstrator that showcases platform-neutral and fallback capabilities
review: This lever governs the tension between technical demonstration and procurement influence, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

name: Regulatory Engagement
consequences: Engaging with regulatory bodies will create a demand for platform-neutral solutions, making it more difficult for suppliers to lock-in users. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Work with regulators to develop and publish new regulations that require Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
Engage with regulatory bodies to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
Develop relationships with key stakeholders within the regulatory community to influence procurement decisions
review: This lever governs the tension between regulation and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

name: Coalition Building
consequences: Building a coalition of civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Develop relationships with key stakeholders within the coalition to influence procurement decisions
Work with coalition partners to develop and publish open standards for fallback authentication that reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Engage with coalition members to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
review: This lever governs the tension between coalition building and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

name: Resilience Framing
consequences: Framing the issue as a resilience problem will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards, making supplier-neutral design harder to exclude. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Develop and publish reports that highlight the risks of supplier concentration and promote platform-neutrality
Engage with stakeholders to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
Work with regulators to develop and publish new regulations that require Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
review: This lever governs the tension between resilience framing and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

name: Technical Research
consequences: Conducting technical research will provide credible evidence that platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models are feasible. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a detailed report on the feasibility of platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models
Explore new technologies and standards that can be used to reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Focus on developing a single, feature-rich demonstrator that showcases platform-neutral and fallback capabilities
review: This lever governs the tension between technical research and procurement influence, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

name: Forklift Strategy
consequences: Implementing a forklift strategy would directly affect the project's ability to adapt to changing requirements, potentially leading to costly rework and delays. This, in turn, could compromise the project's overall timeline and budget.
options: Develop a robust and iterative approach to requirements gathering, ensuring that changes are incorporated seamlessly into the project plan.
Establish a clear and transparent change management process, enabling stakeholders to provide input and feedback throughout the project lifecycle.
Implement a flexible and adaptive project structure, allowing for easy reconfiguration in response to changing requirements or priorities.
review: This lever governs the tension between rigidity and flexibility, but the options overlook potential scalability issues with iterative approaches.

name: Stakeholder Engagement
consequences: Effective stakeholder engagement is crucial for the project's success, as it can influence the adoption of platform-neutral solutions and secure buy-in from key stakeholders. Inadequate engagement may lead to resistance or even opposition from influential stakeholders.
options: Develop a comprehensive stakeholder analysis, identifying key influencers and their interests in order to tailor engagement strategies accordingly.
Establish a transparent and inclusive communication plan, ensuring that all stakeholders are informed and involved throughout the project lifecycle.
Create a dedicated stakeholder management team, responsible for building relationships and addressing concerns in real-time.
review: This lever governs the tension between information sharing and control, but the options overlook potential power dynamics with influential stakeholders.

name: Digital Identity Standards
consequences: Developing standards for digital identity is critical to ensuring interoperability and security across different platforms. Inadequate or inconsistent standards may lead to fragmentation and reduced confidence in the digital identity infrastructure.
options: Establish a clear and comprehensive set of standards, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Develop a modular and flexible standards framework, allowing for easy adaptation and extension in response to changing requirements or emerging technologies.
Create a dedicated standards committee, responsible for reviewing and updating the standards in real-time, in collaboration with relevant stakeholders.
review: This lever governs the tension between standardization and flexibility, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between security and usability.

name: Procurement Framework
consequences: Establishing a procurement framework that prioritizes platform-neutrality is essential for ensuring the long-term sustainability of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or biased frameworks may lead to continued dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
options: Develop a transparent and inclusive procurement process, ensuring that all stakeholders are involved in the selection of vendors and technologies.
Establish clear criteria for vendor evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated procurement team, responsible for managing the procurement process and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
review: This lever governs the tension between openness and control, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest with influential vendors.

name: Cybersecurity Posture
consequences: Maintaining a strong cybersecurity posture is critical to ensuring the integrity and confidentiality of digital identity data. Inadequate or complacent approaches may lead to security breaches and compromised trust in the infrastructure.
options: Develop a robust and proactive cybersecurity strategy, incorporating best practices from organizations such as NIST and ISO.
Establish clear incident response procedures, ensuring that all stakeholders are prepared to respond quickly and effectively in the event of a security breach.
Create a dedicated cybersecurity team, responsible for monitoring and addressing potential threats in real-time.
review: This lever governs the tension between security and convenience, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between risk tolerance and compliance requirements.

name: Partnership Strategy
consequences: Building strategic partnerships is essential for ensuring the long-term sustainability of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or ineffective partnerships may lead to continued dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
options: Develop a clear and comprehensive partnership strategy, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Establish clear criteria for partner evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated partnership team, responsible for managing partnerships and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
review: This lever governs the tension between openness and control, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest with influential partners.

name: Innovation Ecosystem
consequences: Fostering an innovation ecosystem is critical to ensuring the continued relevance and effectiveness of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or stagnant ecosystems may lead to reduced confidence in the infrastructure.
options: Develop a clear and comprehensive innovation strategy, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Establish clear criteria for innovation evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated innovation team, responsible for managing the innovation process and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
review: This lever governs the tension between stability and change, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between risk tolerance and regulatory requirements.

name: Platform-Independent Governance Framework
consequences: Establishing a platform-independent governance framework would directly affect the way Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is managed and regulated, potentially leading to more autonomy and control for the country. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of coordination and standardization among different stakeholders.
options: Implement a decentralized governance model with rotating leadership
Establish a permanent government agency responsible for overseeing digital identity matters
Create an industry-led consortium to develop and enforce standards
review: This lever governs the tension between centralized control and distributed autonomy, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest and accountability mechanisms.

name: Digital Identity Standards Harmonization
consequences: Harmonizing digital identity standards would have a direct impact on the interoperability and compatibility of different systems, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new barriers for innovative solutions that don't conform to standardized approaches.
options: Develop a set of open standards for digital identity verification
Create a certification program for vendors who meet specific standards
Establish a reference implementation for digital identity protocols
review: This lever governs the tension between standardization and innovation, but the options overlook potential lock-in effects.

name: Public-Private Partnership Model
consequences: Developing a public-private partnership model would have a direct impact on the way Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is funded and maintained, potentially leading to increased investment and expertise. However, this might also create new conflicts of interest and accountability challenges.
options: Create a joint venture between government agencies and private companies
Establish a public-private funding mechanism for digital identity initiatives
Develop a co-creation model where stakeholders collaborate on solution development
review: This lever governs the tension between public and private interests, but the options overlook potential power imbalances.

name: Digital Identity Skills Development
consequences: Investing in digital identity skills development would have a direct impact on the capacity and expertise of Denmark's workforce, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing technologies and requirements.
options: Develop training programs for government employees and private sector workers
Create a certification program for digital identity professionals
Establish an industry-led skills development initiative
review: This lever governs the tension between workforce capacity and technological change, but the options overlook potential skill obsolescence.

name: Digital Identity Data Governance
consequences: Developing a data governance framework for digital identity would have a direct impact on the way personal data is collected, stored, and used, potentially leading to increased trust and reduced risks. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of balancing individual rights with national security concerns.
options: Establish a data protection authority responsible for overseeing digital identity data
Create a data sharing framework between government agencies and private companies
Develop guidelines for responsible data practices in digital identity
review: This lever governs the tension between personal data protection and national security, but the options overlook potential trade-offs.

name: Digital Identity Procurement Reform
consequences: Reforming Denmark's procurement process for digital identity solutions would have a direct impact on the way government agencies acquire and implement new technologies, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing requirements and supplier relationships.
options: Streamline the procurement process through electronic tendering
Create a centralized procurement agency for digital identity solutions
Develop a co-creation model where stakeholders collaborate on solution development
review: This lever governs the tension between efficiency and adaptability, but the options overlook potential supplier resistance.

consequences: Launching an awareness campaign for digital identity would have a direct impact on public understanding and engagement with digital identity issues, potentially leading to increased trust and reduced risks. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of balancing information dissemination with potential misinformation.
options: Develop educational materials for schools and community programs
Create a public awareness campaign through social media and advertising
Establish an industry-led initiative to promote digital identity literacy

options: Establish a research institute focused on digital identity
Create a collaborative research program with industry partners
Develop guidelines for responsible innovation in digital identity
